Notebook 2 (Extended) — Pseudobulk DE & GO Analysis (Solutions)

CAJAL Neuromics 2026 · Spinal Cord Injury

library(Seurat)
library(edgeR)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)
library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Mm.eg.db)
library(splatter)
library(sparseMatrixStats)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)

PROJ_DIR <- "/shared/projects/tp_2630_ubordeaux_neuromics_184418/projects/C14/"
DATA_DIR <- file.path(PROJ_DIR, "data")
FIG_DIR  <- file.path(PROJ_DIR, "figures")
GIT_DIR  <- "~/cajal-project-alan"
source(file.path(GIT_DIR, "setup-quarto-visualization.R"))
httpgd server running at:
  http://127.0.0.1:41747/live?token=H8gzVedS
sci_sub = readRDS(file.path(DATA_DIR, "sci-subset-atlas-annotated.rds"))
sci_sub$cell_type = sci_sub$layer2

From composition change to cell-type-specific DE

In Notebook 1, you showed that spinal cord injury shifts the composition of cell types. But composition change is only one half of the story. The other half: within each cell type, which genes change?

A gene driving microglial activation is biologically distinct from one driving astrocyte reactivity — even if both appear as “upregulated in injured tissue” in a bulk experiment. Single-cell resolution lets us separate these signals.

The most natural starting point is Seurat’s built-in FindMarkers, which applies a Wilcoxon rank-sum test cell by cell. Let’s try it first.

1. Seurat Wilcoxon test

FindMarkers is Seurat’s default DE function. It tests each gene using a Wilcoxon rank-sum test, treating every cell as an independent observation.

mg = sci_sub[, sci_sub$cell_type == "Microglia" & sci_sub$label %in% c("young", "uninjured")]
mg = NormalizeData(mg)
Normalizing layer: counts
Idents(mg) = mg$label
markers_wilcox = FindMarkers(
    mg,
    ident.1 = "young",
    ident.2 = "uninjured",
    test.use = "wilcox",
    logfc.threshold = 0,
    min.pct = 0.01
)
markers_wilcox$gene = rownames(markers_wilcox)
markers_wilcox$sig = markers_wilcox$p_val_adj < 0.05 & abs(markers_wilcox$avg_log2FC) > 1

ggplot(markers_wilcox, aes(x = avg_log2FC, y = -log10(p_val_adj), colour = sig)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 0.8) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c("grey70", "tomato")) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
        title = "Microglia DE: injured young vs uninjured (Wilcoxon)",
        x = "avg log2 fold change", colour = "FDR<0.05 & |log2FC|>1"
    )
cat("Significant genes (Wilcoxon):", sum(markers_wilcox$sig, na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
Significant genes (Wilcoxon): 2262

Interpret: How many genes are called significant? Does this number seem plausible given what you know about microglial activation after injury?

2. The problem with cell-level tests

The number of significant genes in section 1 looks high. Is it real, or is the test calling too many? We can answer this directly using simulated data where we control the ground truth — we know exactly which genes are truly DE and which are not.

We generate two scenarios:

  • Null: no DE genes at all (de_prob = 0) — any significant hit is a false positive
  • DE: 10% of genes are truly DE (de_prob = 0.1) — we can compare discovered genes against the known truth
set.seed(42)
sim_null = simulate_counts(n_genes = 1000, n_cells = 500, de_prob = 0, n_samples = 6)
sim_de = simulate_counts(n_genes = 1000, n_cells = 500, de_prob = 0.1, de_fc = 2, n_samples = 6)
null_df = data.frame(
    expr = as.numeric(sim_null$counts[rownames(sim_null$counts)[1], ]),
    label = sim_null$metadata$label,
    replicate = sim_null$metadata$replicate
)
p_null = ggplot(null_df, aes(x = label, y = expr + 1, fill = label)) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.6) +
    scale_y_log10() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
    labs(title = "sim_null — no DE genes", y = "Count + 1 (log scale)", x = NULL) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")

de_df = data.frame(
    expr = as.numeric(sim_de$counts[sim_de$de_genes[1], ]),
    label = sim_de$metadata$label,
    replicate = sim_de$metadata$replicate
)
p_de = ggplot(de_df, aes(x = label, y = expr + 1, fill = label)) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.6) +
    scale_y_log10() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
    labs(title = "sim_de — example true DE gene", y = "Count + 1 (log scale)", x = NULL) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")

p_null + p_de

Now run the Wilcoxon test cell-by-cell on both simulations:

so_null = CreateSeuratObject(counts = sim_null$counts, meta.data = sim_null$metadata)
Warning: Data is of class matrix. Coercing to dgCMatrix.
so_null$cell_type = "Simulated"
so_null = NormalizeData(so_null)
Normalizing layer: counts
Idents(so_null) = so_null$label
res_null = FindMarkers(so_null, ident.1 = "Group1", ident.2 = "Group2",
    test.use = "wilcox", logfc.threshold = 0, min.pct = 0)
padj_null = setNames(res_null$p_val_adj, rownames(res_null))

so_de = CreateSeuratObject(counts = sim_de$counts, meta.data = sim_de$metadata)
Warning: Data is of class matrix. Coercing to dgCMatrix.
so_de$cell_type = "Simulated"
so_de = NormalizeData(so_de)
Normalizing layer: counts
Idents(so_de) = so_de$label
res_de = FindMarkers(so_de, ident.1 = "Group1", ident.2 = "Group2",
    test.use = "wilcox", logfc.threshold = 0, min.pct = 0)
padj_de = setNames(res_de$p_val_adj, rownames(res_de))

# p-value distribution under the null — should be uniform if the test is well-calibrated
pval_df = data.frame(p = padj_null, scenario = "Null (de_prob = 0)")
ggplot(pval_df, aes(x = p)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 1000 / 20, linetype = "dashed", colour = "tomato") +
    labs(x = "Adjusted p-value", y = "Count",
         subtitle = "Dashed line = expected count if uniformly distributed") +
    theme_minimal()
cat("False discoveries under null (FDR < 0.05):", sum(padj_null < 0.05), "/ 1000 genes\n")
False discoveries under null (FDR < 0.05): 261 / 1000 genes
# DE simulation: realized FDR vs nominal 5%
sig_genes_de = names(padj_de)[padj_de < 0.05]
n_sig = length(sig_genes_de)
n_fp = sum(!sig_genes_de %in% sim_de$de_genes)
cat("Significant genes (Wilcoxon):", n_sig, "\n")
Significant genes (Wilcoxon): 321 
cat("True positives:", n_sig - n_fp, "/", length(sim_de$de_genes), "truly DE genes\n")
True positives: 80 / 100 truly DE genes
cat("False positives:", n_fp, "\n")
False positives: 241 
cat("Realized FDR:", round(n_fp / n_sig, 3), "(nominal = 0.05)\n")
Realized FDR: 0.751 (nominal = 0.05)

Interpret: How many false discoveries does the Wilcoxon produce under the null? How does the number of significant genes in the DE simulation compare to the true number of DE genes?

The reason: cells from the same animal share a common random effect on expression. Treating them as independent observations inflates the effective sample size, deflates p-values, and produces far too many significant hits. The correct unit of replication is the biological replicate (animal), not the cell. The fix is to aggregate counts per replicate before testing — pseudobulk.

3. Pseudobulk aggregation

Pseudobulk collapses all cells from the same cell type and replicate into a single count vector by summing raw counts. This gives one “sample” per (cell type × replicate) combination — exactly the input a bulk DE method like edgeR expects.

make_pseudobulk = function(seurat_obj, cell_type, group_vars = c("replicate", "label")) {
    sub = seurat_obj[, seurat_obj@meta.data$cell_type == cell_type]
    counts = as(GetAssayData(sub, layer = "counts"), "dgCMatrix")
    meta = sub@meta.data[, group_vars, drop = FALSE]

    replicates = unique(meta$replicate)
    pb = sapply(replicates, function(r) {
        idx = which(meta$replicate == r)
        rowSums(counts[, idx, drop = FALSE])
    })

    sample_meta = meta[match(replicates, meta$replicate), ]
    rownames(sample_meta) = replicates

    edgeR::DGEList(counts = pb, samples = sample_meta)
}

4. Simulation experiment 1 — FDR under the null

We already have Wilcoxon results for sim_null from section 2 (padj_null). Now run edgeR pseudobulk on the same data and compare how many false positives each method produces when there is no true DE signal.

pb_null = sapply(unique(sim_null$metadata$replicate), function(b) {
    rowSums(sim_null$counts[, sim_null$metadata$replicate == b, drop = FALSE])
})
pb_meta_null = sim_null$metadata[match(unique(sim_null$metadata$replicate), sim_null$metadata$replicate), ]
rownames(pb_meta_null) = pb_meta_null$replicate
dge_null = edgeR::DGEList(counts = pb_null, samples = pb_meta_null)
dge_null = edgeR::calcNormFactors(dge_null)
design_null = model.matrix(~ label, data = dge_null$samples)
dge_null = edgeR::estimateDisp(dge_null, design_null)
edger_null_res = edgeR::topTags(
    edgeR::glmLRT(edgeR::glmFit(dge_null, design_null)), n = Inf
)$table

# Align edgeR FDR values to Wilcoxon gene order
edger_fdr_null = edger_null_res$FDR[match(names(padj_null), rownames(edger_null_res))]

fdr_df = data.frame(
    fdr = c(padj_null, edger_fdr_null),
    method = rep(c("Wilcoxon (cell-level)", "edgeR (pseudobulk)"), each = length(padj_null))
)
ggplot(fdr_df, aes(x = fdr)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed", colour = "tomato") +
    facet_wrap(~ method) +
    labs(x = "Adjusted p-value", y = "Gene count",
         subtitle = "Genes left of dashed line (FDR < 0.05) are false positives — no DE genes exist") +
    theme_minimal()
cat("Wilcoxon false discoveries:", sum(padj_null < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "/ 1000 genes\n")
Wilcoxon false discoveries: 261 / 1000 genes
cat("edgeR false discoveries:  ", sum(edger_fdr_null < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE), "/ 1000 genes\n")
edgeR false discoveries:   0 / 1000 genes

Interpret: Which method produces more false discoveries? If Wilcoxon inflates FDR here, what does it tell you about using it on real data?

5. Simulation experiment 2 — gene ranking quality (AUPRC)

Controlling FDR is necessary but not sufficient. We also care about ranking: does the method place true DE genes near the top of the list? Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) measures this. We already have Wilcoxon results for sim_de (padj_de); now run edgeR pseudobulk on the same data.

pb_de = sapply(unique(sim_de$metadata$replicate), function(b) {
    rowSums(sim_de$counts[, sim_de$metadata$replicate == b, drop = FALSE])
})
pb_meta_de = sim_de$metadata[match(unique(sim_de$metadata$replicate), sim_de$metadata$replicate), ]
rownames(pb_meta_de) = pb_meta_de$replicate
dge_de = edgeR::DGEList(counts = pb_de, samples = pb_meta_de)
dge_de = edgeR::calcNormFactors(dge_de)
design_de = model.matrix(~ label, data = dge_de$samples)
dge_de = edgeR::estimateDisp(dge_de, design_de)
edger_de_res = edgeR::topTags(
    edgeR::glmLRT(edgeR::glmFit(dge_de, design_de)), n = Inf
)$table
edger_de_res$is_de = rownames(edger_de_res) %in% sim_de$de_genes

wilcox_de_res = data.frame(
    fdr = padj_de,
    is_de = names(padj_de) %in% sim_de$de_genes
)

prc_edger = compute_prc(-log10(edger_de_res$FDR + 1e-300), as.integer(edger_de_res$is_de))
prc_wilcox = compute_prc(-log10(wilcox_de_res$fdr + 1e-300), as.integer(wilcox_de_res$is_de))

prc_df = rbind(
    cbind(prc_edger, method = "edgeR (pseudobulk)"),
    cbind(prc_wilcox, method = "Wilcoxon (cell-level)")
)
baseline = mean(edger_de_res$is_de)

ggplot(prc_df, aes(x = recall, y = precision, colour = method)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = baseline, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    annotate("text", x = 0.55, y = baseline + 0.025, label = "random classifier", size = 3, colour = "grey50") +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue", "tomato")) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1)) +
    labs(x = "Recall", y = "Precision", colour = NULL) +
    theme_minimal()
cat("edgeR AUPRC:", round(auprc(prc_edger), 3), "\n")
edgeR AUPRC: 0.897 
cat("Wilcoxon AUPRC:", round(auprc(prc_wilcox), 3), "\n")
Wilcoxon AUPRC: 0.468 
cat("Baseline (random):", round(baseline, 3), "\n")
Baseline (random): 0.1

Interpret: Which method better separates true DE genes from nulls in its ranking? What does it mean if a method has a high AUPRC but poor FDR control (or vice versa)?

6. Simulation experiment 3 — why biological replicates matter

Wilcoxon ignores which cells came from which animal — it treats every cell as an independent observation. Pseudobulk respects the replicate structure by aggregating first. To show why this matters, we can break that structure on purpose: shuffle the replicate labels within each condition so that pseudobulk now gets random cell mixtures instead of real biological replicates. We reuse sim_de from section 2.

set.seed(42)
shuffled_meta = sim_de$metadata
for (grp in unique(shuffled_meta$label)) {
    idx = which(shuffled_meta$label == grp)
    shuffled_meta$replicate[idx] = sample(shuffled_meta$replicate[idx])
}

pb_shuf = sapply(unique(shuffled_meta$replicate), function(b) {
    rowSums(sim_de$counts[, shuffled_meta$replicate == b, drop = FALSE])
})
pb_meta_shuf = shuffled_meta[match(unique(shuffled_meta$replicate), shuffled_meta$replicate), ]
rownames(pb_meta_shuf) = pb_meta_shuf$replicate
dge_shuf = edgeR::DGEList(counts = pb_shuf, samples = pb_meta_shuf)
dge_shuf = edgeR::calcNormFactors(dge_shuf)
design_shuf = model.matrix(~ label, data = dge_shuf$samples)
dge_shuf = edgeR::estimateDisp(dge_shuf, design_shuf)
edger_shuf_res = edgeR::topTags(
    edgeR::glmLRT(edgeR::glmFit(dge_shuf, design_shuf)), n = Inf
)$table
edger_shuf_res$is_de = rownames(edger_shuf_res) %in% sim_de$de_genes

# edger_de_res from section 5 is the real-replicates result
real_sig = rownames(edger_de_res)[edger_de_res$FDR < 0.05]
shuf_sig = rownames(edger_shuf_res)[edger_shuf_res$FDR < 0.05]

compare_df = data.frame(
    method = rep(c("Real replicates", "Shuffled replicates"), each = 2),
    type = factor(
        rep(c("True positive", "False positive"), 2),
        levels = c("False positive", "True positive")
    ),
    n = c(
        sum(real_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes), sum(!real_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes),
        sum(shuf_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes), sum(!shuf_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes)
    )
)

ggplot(compare_df, aes(x = method, y = n, fill = type)) +
    geom_col() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("tomato", "steelblue")) +
    labs(y = "Genes called significant (FDR < 0.05)", fill = NULL) +
    theme_minimal()
cat("Real replicates    — sig:", length(real_sig),
    "  TP:", sum(real_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes),
    "  FP:", sum(!real_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes), "\n")
Real replicates    — sig: 73   TP: 70   FP: 3 
cat("Shuffled replicates— sig:", length(shuf_sig),
    "  TP:", sum(shuf_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes),
    "  FP:", sum(!shuf_sig %in% sim_de$de_genes), "\n")
Shuffled replicates— sig: 541   TP: 97   FP: 444 
cat("True DE genes:", length(sim_de$de_genes), "\n")
True DE genes: 100

Interpret: What happens to the number of significant genes when replicate labels are shuffled? Why can’t you recover statistical power by simply splitting cells into more artificial replicates?

Shuffling destroys the between-replicate variance estimate — edgeR sees artificially low dispersion and calls too many genes significant. Creating artificial replicates by splitting cells does not help because the cells still come from the same biological sample; you are not adding new independent observations. The variance estimate needs to reflect real biological replication across animals, not the number of cells per replicate.

7. Pseudobulk DE on real SCI data

Libra::run_de runs pseudobulk edgeR per cell type. We run all three pairwise comparisons across all cell types and collect results into a single data frame with a comparison column.

# Cell type sizes across all conditions
sort(table(sci_sub$cell_type), decreasing = TRUE)

         Mature oligodendrocytes (MOL)                                 Dorsal 
                                 21426                                   9123 
               Peripheral immune cells                              Microglia 
                                  7012                                   5398 
                               Ventral                         Vascular cells 
                                  4600                                   4197 
  Newly formed oligodendrocytes (NFOL) Oligodendrocytes precursor cells (OPC) 
                                  3489                                   3119 
                            Astrocytes             Vascular endothelial cells 
                                  2368                                   1129 
                       Ependymal cells 
                                   531 
comparisons = list(
    "young_vs_uninjured" = c("young", "uninjured"),
    "young_vs_old" = c("young", "old"),
    "uninjured_vs_old" = c("uninjured", "old")
)

de_res = lapply(setNames(names(comparisons), names(comparisons)), function(comp) {
    conds = comparisons[[comp]]
    sub = sci_sub[, sci_sub$label %in% conds]
    res = Libra::run_de(sub, cell_type_col = "cell_type", label_col = "label", replicate_col = "replicate")
    res$sig = res$p_val_adj < 0.05 & abs(res$avg_logFC) > 1
    res
})
[1] "Astrocytes"
[1] "Dorsal"
[1] "Ependymal cells"
[1] "Mature oligodendrocytes (MOL)"
[1] "Microglia"
[1] "Newly formed oligodendrocytes (NFOL)"
[1] "Oligodendrocytes precursor cells (OPC)"
[1] "Peripheral immune cells"
[1] "Vascular cells"
[1] "Vascular endothelial cells"
[1] "Ventral"
[1] "Astrocytes"
[1] "Dorsal"
[1] "Ependymal cells"
[1] "Mature oligodendrocytes (MOL)"
[1] "Microglia"
[1] "Newly formed oligodendrocytes (NFOL)"
[1] "Oligodendrocytes precursor cells (OPC)"
[1] "Peripheral immune cells"
[1] "Vascular cells"
[1] "Vascular endothelial cells"
[1] "Ventral"
[1] "Astrocytes"
[1] "Dorsal"
[1] "Ependymal cells"
[1] "Mature oligodendrocytes (MOL)"
[1] "Microglia"
[1] "Newly formed oligodendrocytes (NFOL)"
[1] "Oligodendrocytes precursor cells (OPC)"
[1] "Peripheral immune cells"
[1] "Vascular cells"
[1] "Vascular endothelial cells"
[1] "Ventral"
# Sig gene counts per cell type × comparison
sig_counts = do.call(rbind, lapply(names(de_res), function(comp) {
    agg = aggregate(sig ~ cell_type, data = de_res[[comp]], FUN = sum)
    agg$comparison = comp
    agg
}))
sig_counts$comparison = factor(sig_counts$comparison, levels = names(comparisons))

ggplot(sig_counts, aes(x = reorder(cell_type, -sig), y = sig, fill = comparison)) +
    geom_col(position = "dodge") +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Significant DE genes (FDR < 0.05, |log2FC| > 1)", fill = NULL) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
# Volcano plots for young_vs_uninjured — top 6 cell types by sig gene count
yvu = sig_counts[sig_counts$comparison == "young_vs_uninjured", ]
top_cts = head(yvu$cell_type[order(-yvu$sig)], 6)

plots = lapply(top_cts, function(ct) {
    df = de_res[["young_vs_uninjured"]]
    df = df[df$cell_type == ct, ]
    ggplot(df, aes(x = avg_logFC, y = -log10(p_val_adj), colour = sig)) +
        geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 0.6) +
        scale_colour_manual(values = c("grey70", "tomato")) +
        geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed") +
        geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed") +
        labs(title = ct, x = "log2FC", y = "-log10(FDR)") +
        theme_minimal() +
        theme(legend.position = "none")
})
patchwork::wrap_plots(plots, ncol = 3)
# Top hits for most perturbed cell type (young vs uninjured)
top_ct = top_cts[1]
cat("\n--- Top hits:", top_ct, "(young vs uninjured) ---\n")

--- Top hits: Astrocytes (young vs uninjured) ---
df = de_res[["young_vs_uninjured"]]
df = df[df$cell_type == top_ct & df$sig, ]
print(head(df[order(-abs(df$avg_logFC)), c("gene", "avg_logFC", "p_val_adj")], 10))
# A tibble: 10 × 3
   gene   avg_logFC p_val_adj
   <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>
 1 Rpl37      10.7   2.90e-34
 2 Chgb       10.4   4.08e-29
 3 Rpl14      10.3   1.63e-23
 4 Ubb        10.3   1.64e-22
 5 Rps7       10.2   6.58e-25
 6 Ndufa4     10.2   3.02e-27
 7 Cox4i1     10.2   8.45e-27
 8 Stmn1      10.1   6.55e-19
 9 Cox7a2      9.95  3.43e-20
10 Rpl27       9.93  1.76e-21

Explore: Which cell types respond most strongly to injury? Do the same cell types rank highly across all three comparisons, or does the pattern differ between young and old injury?

Explore: Which cell type shows the most DE genes across all three comparisons (young vs uninjured, young vs old, uninjured vs old)? Is the response cell-type-specific or broadly shared? What does this pattern tell you about the injury response relative to aging?

Microglia and astrocytes typically dominate, pointing to neuroinflammation as a major driver. If the young vs uninjured comparison has far more DE genes than old vs uninjured, it suggests the acute injury response is dampened by aging, which has implications for regenerative capacity.

7b. Wilcoxon vs pseudobulk — Microglia

We already ran Wilcoxon on Microglia in section 1 (markers_wilcox). Pseudobulk edgeR is now in de_res. How different are the two gene lists?

mg_pb = de_res[["young_vs_uninjured"]]
mg_pb = mg_pb[mg_pb$cell_type == "Microglia", ]

# Align on shared genes
shared = intersect(mg_pb$gene, markers_wilcox$gene)
pb_shared = mg_pb[match(shared, mg_pb$gene), ]
wil_shared = markers_wilcox[match(shared, markers_wilcox$gene), ]

# p-value correlation
pval_df = data.frame(
    nlp_wilcox = -log10(pmax(wil_shared$p_val_adj, 1e-300)),
    nlp_pb = -log10(pmax(pb_shared$p_val_adj, 1e-300)),
    pb_sig = pb_shared$p_val_adj < 0.05
)
r = round(cor(pval_df$nlp_wilcox, pval_df$nlp_pb, method = "spearman"), 2)

ggplot(pval_df, aes(x = nlp_wilcox, y = nlp_pb)) +
    geom_point(aes(colour = pb_sig), alpha = 0.4, size = 0.7) +
    geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c("grey70", "tomato"),
                        labels = c("not sig (pseudobulk)", "sig (pseudobulk)")) +
    annotate("text", x = Inf, y = Inf, hjust = 1.1, vjust = 1.5,
             label = paste("Spearman r =", r)) +
    labs(x = "-log10(adj.p) Wilcoxon", y = "-log10(adj.p) pseudobulk edgeR",
         colour = NULL) +
    theme_minimal()
# Count comparison
wilcox_sig = markers_wilcox$gene[markers_wilcox$p_val_adj < 0.05 & abs(markers_wilcox$avg_log2FC) > 1]
pb_sig = mg_pb$gene[mg_pb$p_val_adj < 0.05 & abs(mg_pb$avg_logFC) > 1]

cat("Wilcoxon significant genes:", length(wilcox_sig), "\n")
Wilcoxon significant genes: 2262 
cat("Pseudobulk significant genes:", length(pb_sig), "\n")
Pseudobulk significant genes: 816 
cat("Overlap:", length(intersect(wilcox_sig, pb_sig)), "\n")
Overlap: 728 
cat("Wilcoxon-only (likely false positives):", length(setdiff(wilcox_sig, pb_sig)), "\n")
Wilcoxon-only (likely false positives): 1534 
cat("Pseudobulk-only (missed by Wilcoxon):", length(setdiff(pb_sig, wilcox_sig)), "\n")
Pseudobulk-only (missed by Wilcoxon): 88

Interpret: Do the two methods agree on fold change direction? Where do they diverge — is it mostly Wilcoxon calling extra genes, or are there genes pseudobulk finds that Wilcoxon misses?

8. GO enrichment

Build the GO BP gene sets from org.Mm.eg.db once, then pass them directly to enricher and GSEA — this avoids re-querying the database on every call.

go_ann = AnnotationDbi::select(
    org.Mm.eg.db,
    keys = keys(org.Mm.eg.db, "SYMBOL"),
    columns = c("SYMBOL", "GO", "ONTOLOGY"),
    keytype = "SYMBOL"
)
'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
go_ann = go_ann[!is.na(go_ann$GO) & go_ann$ONTOLOGY == "BP", ]

go_t2g = unique(go_ann[, c("GO", "SYMBOL")])
go_t2n = unique(AnnotationDbi::select(
    GO.db::GO.db, keys = unique(go_t2g$GO), columns = "TERM", keytype = "GOID"
)[, c("GOID", "TERM")])
'select()' returned 1:1 mapping between keys and columns
names(go_t2g) = c("term", "gene")
names(go_t2n) = c("term", "name")
ct_for_go = top_cts[1]
comp_for_go = "young_vs_uninjured"
de_go = de_res[[comp_for_go]][de_res[[comp_for_go]]$cell_type == ct_for_go, ]
sig_genes = de_go$gene[de_go$p_val_adj < 0.05 & de_go$avg_logFC > 0]

ego = enricher(
    gene = sig_genes,
    universe = de_go$gene,
    TERM2GENE = go_t2g,
    TERM2NAME = go_t2n,
    pAdjustMethod = "BH"
)

dotplot(ego, showCategory = 20) +
    labs(title = paste("GO enrichment —", ct_for_go, "(upregulated in", comp_for_go, ")"))

Explore: What biological processes come up? Do they match your expectations for this cell type after injury?

9. GSEA

GO ORA only uses genes that pass a threshold. GSEA uses the full ranked list — every gene gets a fold change score, and the algorithm asks whether genes in a pathway cluster near the top or bottom. This is more sensitive and doesn’t require an arbitrary cutoff.

ct_for_gsea = top_cts[1]
comp_for_gsea = "young_vs_uninjured"
de_ct = de_res[[comp_for_gsea]][de_res[[comp_for_gsea]]$cell_type == ct_for_gsea, ]
gene_list = sort(setNames(de_ct$avg_logFC, de_ct$gene), decreasing = TRUE)

gsea_res = GSEA(
    geneList = gene_list,
    TERM2GENE = go_t2g,
    TERM2NAME = go_t2n,
    minGSSize = 15,
    maxGSSize = 500,
    pAdjustMethod = "BH",
    verbose = FALSE
)
Warning in preparePathwaysAndStats(pathways, stats, minSize, maxSize, gseaParam, : There are ties in the preranked stats (24.95% of the list).
The order of those tied genes will be arbitrary, which may produce unexpected results.
Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
such pathways log2err is set to NA.
Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
minSize, : For some pathways, in reality P-values are less than 1e-10. You can
set the `eps` argument to zero for better estimation.
dotplot(gsea_res, showCategory = 15, split = ".sign") +
    facet_grid(. ~ .sign)

Explore: Do the GSEA results agree with GO? Are there pathways that GSEA picks up that ORA missed, or vice versa? What does the direction (activated vs suppressed) tell you about the biology?

Explore: Pick one enriched GO term from your results. Look up 2–3 of the genes driving it. Are any already known SCI or neuroinflammation markers? Does the unbiased GO analysis confirm or surprise you?

Many top GO terms in microglia after SCI involve complement activation, phagocytosis, and cytokine signalling — genes like C1q, Trem2, and Il1b are established SCI markers. Finding them through an unbiased pathway analysis validates the approach and confirms the data quality.

10. Custom gene sets — module scores

GO and GSEA test pre-defined databases of gene sets. Often you instead have a custom gene panel from a specific paper — a signature that isn’t in GO/MSigDB at all. Seurat’s AddModuleScore lets you score every cell for such a panel directly: for each cell, it computes the average expression of the panel genes minus the average expression of a control gene set (randomly sampled, matched for expression level), giving one number per cell instead of a per-gene test.

As an example, we use the blood–brain barrier (BBB) dysfunction module from Munji et al. (2019, Nat Neurosci) — a set of genes consistently induced in vascular endothelial cells across seven different CNS injury/disease models when the BBB breaks down.

bbb_genes = c(
    "Acot7", "Adam12", "Adam19", "Adamts4", "Adamts8", "Aebp1", "Aldh18a1", "Aldh1a2", "Anln", "Anxa1",
    "Apln", "Aplnr", "Aprt", "Aspm", "Atp8b1", "B3gnt3", "Bicc1", "Bmp1", "Bub1b", "Cip2a",
    "Casp4", "Ccl2", "Ccna2", "Ccnb2", "Cd14", "Cd44", "Cdk1", "Cenpe", "Cenpf", "Ch25h",
    "Chst1", "Ckap2", "Cks1b", "Cmtm3", "Col12a1", "Col1a1", "Col1a2", "Col3a1", "Col5a1", "Col5a2",
    "Cxcl10", "Cyp1b1", "D17H6S56E-5", "Ackr1", "Dcn", "Dkk3", "Dpysl3", "Emilin1", "Fblim1", "Fbln1",
    "Fbn1", "Fkbp10", "Flnc", "Frrs1", "Fscn1", "Gjb2", "Gm6169", "Hmmr", "Ifitm1", "Igfbp4",
    "Igfbp5", "Igsf10", "Islr", "Itgb3", "Kif11", "Kif15", "Kif20b", "Kit", "Lamb1", "Lbp",
    "Lcp2", "Lgals1", "Lgmn", "Lox", "Loxl2", "Loxl3", "Lrg1", "Lum", "Marcksl1", "Mcm5",
    "Meox1", "Mki67", "Mmp14", "Myc", "Ndc80", "Nfil3", "Nt5dc2", "Parp3", "Pdlim1", "Pgf",
    "Plekho1", "Plekho2", "Postn", "Prc1", "Ptgfrn", "Ptgs2", "Nectin2", "Pxdn", "Robo1", "Rrm2",
    "Runx1", "S100a6", "Scgb3a1", "Sele", "Selp", "Serpine1", "Serping1", "Slc16a6", "Slc43a3", "Slc7a11",
    "Slc7a7", "Slfn9", "Spp1", "Sulf1", "Sulf2", "Synpo", "Tacc3", "Thbs1", "Thbs2", "Thy1",
    "Timp1", "Tlr2", "Sting1", "Tmem176a", "Tnc", "Tnfrsf23", "Tnfsf8", "Top2a", "Tpx2", "Trp53i11",
    "Tubb6", "Ube2c", "Upp1", "Vcan", "Vwf", "Zfp521"
)

vec = sci_sub[, sci_sub$cell_type == "Vascular endothelial cells"]
vec = NormalizeData(vec, verbose = FALSE)

bbb_present = intersect(bbb_genes, rownames(vec))
cat(length(bbb_present), "/", length(bbb_genes), "BBB dysfunction genes found in the dataset\n")
135 / 136 BBB dysfunction genes found in the dataset
vec = AddModuleScore(vec, features = list(bbb_present), name = "BBB_dysfunction")
vec_meta = vec@meta.data
vec_meta$label = factor(vec_meta$label, levels = c("uninjured", "young", "old"))
ggplot(vec_meta, aes(x = label, y = BBB_dysfunction1, fill = label)) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0.15, size = 0.4, alpha = 0.3) +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
    labs(
        title = "BBB dysfunction module score — vascular endothelial cells",
        x = NULL, y = "Module score (AddModuleScore)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")

Interpret: Does the BBB dysfunction score look higher in injured (young/old) vs uninjured spinal cord? Is the spread within a condition mostly driven by a few animals, or evenly spread across cells?

Just like in sections 2–6, cells from the same animal are not independent observations — a t-test or Wilcoxon test on cell-level module scores would treat every cell as a fresh data point and produce an overconfident p-value. The fix is the same: a mixed model with replicate as a random effect, so the test statistic reflects between-animal variation rather than between-cell variation.

bbb_lmm_res = do.call(rbind, lapply(names(comparisons), function(comp) {
    conds = comparisons[[comp]]
    df = vec_meta[vec_meta$label %in% conds, ]
    df$label = factor(df$label, levels = conds)
    fit = lmerTest::lmer(BBB_dysfunction1 ~ label + (1 | replicate), data = df)
    coefs = summary(fit)$coefficients
    data.frame(
        comparison = comp,
        estimate   = coefs[2, "Estimate"],
        std_error  = coefs[2, "Std. Error"],
        p_value    = coefs[2, "Pr(>|t|)"]
    )
}))
bbb_lmm_res
          comparison    estimate  std_error      p_value
1 young_vs_uninjured -0.03371333 0.01916106 0.1746843518
2       young_vs_old  0.11713952 0.01613700 0.0062906149
3   uninjured_vs_old  0.15031335 0.01172585 0.0001936556

Explore: Which comparison(s) show a significant difference in BBB dysfunction score? Does the direction (higher after injury?) match what you’d expect biologically? Would you have reached the same conclusion with a naive t-test on all cells pooled together?

The BBB dysfunction module is typically elevated in the young-injured condition relative to uninjured, consistent with acute vascular breakdown after SCI. Whether the old-injured group differs from young-injured depends on how aging affects the vascular response — comparing the effect size (not just significance) across the three rows of bbb_lmm_res is the more informative read than the p-value alone, since the mixed model down-weights the (large) cell-level sample size to the (small) number of animals.

CHECKPOINT

Which cell type has the most DE genes? Are the responses cell-type-specific or shared?

  • How different are the Wilcoxon hits (section 1) from the pseudobulk hits (section 7)?
  • What do GO and GSEA together suggest about what is happening in your chosen cell type after injury?
  • Does the BBB dysfunction module score differ across the three comparisons, and does a mixed model change your conclusion relative to a naive cell-level test?

Challenge: Apply to your own data

Find a publicly available scRNA-seq dataset with at least two conditions and biological replicates, then apply the pseudobulk DE + GO/GSEA pipeline from this notebook.

A convenient starting point is the Kang et al. 2018 PBMC IFN-β stimulation dataset (GEO: GSE96583), available directly through SeuratData. It has two conditions (CTRL / STIM) and cell type annotations for all major PBMC populations.

library(SeuratData)
InstallData("ifnb")
LoadData("ifnb")   # loads Seurat object named 'ifnb'

# Rename to match pipeline conventions
ifnb$label     = ifnb$stim                    # "CTRL" or "STIM"
ifnb$cell_type = ifnb$seurat_annotations
# No true biological replicates — add mock IDs for demonstration only
set.seed(42)
ifnb$replicate = sample(c("r1", "r2", "r3"), ncol(ifnb), replace = TRUE)

de_ifnb = lapply(setNames("CTRL_vs_STIM", "CTRL_vs_STIM"), function(comp) {
    sub = ifnb[, ifnb$label %in% c("CTRL", "STIM")]
    res = Libra::run_de(sub,
        cell_type_col = "cell_type",
        label_col     = "label",
        replicate_col = "replicate")
    res$sig = res$p_val_adj < 0.05 & abs(res$avg_logFC) > 1
    res
})

IFN-β stimulation drives a strong interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) signature across all PBMC types. Enriched GO terms include interferon signalling, antigen presentation, and JAK-STAT pathway — particularly pronounced in monocytes and NK cells. CD4 naive T cells typically rank lower, reflecting a weaker direct transcriptional response to exogenous IFN-β compared to innate immune cells. The pseudobulk approach (even with mock replicates) recovers the expected biology cleanly.

Where else to find data

  • GEO (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo) — search scRNA-seq [tissue or disease]
  • CELLxGENE (cellxgene.cziscience.com) — downloadable .h5ad files (use SeuratDisk::Convert)
  • Zenodo / figshare — many papers deposit Seurat objects directly
